symptoms.symptom_name: High-energy blunt occipital head trauma (fall ~1.5 m onto concrete)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Mechanism with significant deceleration and occipital impact is a recognised high-risk criterion for intracranial injury.
Prolonged LOC indicates significant force and raises the probability of structural brain injury.
Brief LOC also occurs in concussion, but does not on its own exclude a structural bleed.
Ongoing amnesia/confusion signals significant brain dysfunction and is a CT-mandating red flag.

symptoms.symptom_name: Repeated forceful vomiting (three episodes) after head injury

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: More than one episode of post-traumatic vomiting is a validated marker of intracranial injury and rising ICP.

symptoms.symptom_name: Progressive decline in conscious level (increasing drowsiness)

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: expanding intracranial haemorrhage with raised ICP / early herniation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A falling conscious level after head injury is the cardinal sign of an expanding lesion and impending herniation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Anisocoria — unilateral fixed, dilating right pupil (lay/video observation)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A blown pupil after head trauma is a classic sign of ipsilateral uncal herniation compressing cranial nerve III.

symptoms.symptom_name: Boggy occipital haematoma at impact site (formal palpation, sealed)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A large boggy scalp haematoma localises significant impact energy and, in adults, associates with underlying injury; a lay 'soft lump' report is admissible in telehealth but formal characterisation is clinician-only.

symptoms.symptom_name: Severe positional dizziness and vomiting on attempting to sit up

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Postural exacerbation of nausea/vomiting is consistent with raised ICP and reinforces the need for ICP-aware positioning.
Absence of anticoagulation slightly lowers bleeding risk but does NOT reduce the urgency given the objective herniation signs; its main value is that no reversal agent is needed.
May confound conscious-level assessment but cannot account for a unilateral fixed pupil or focal signs.

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: expanding intracranial haemorrhage with raised ICP / early herniation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Attributing the picture to alcohol is a dangerous distractor — the focal neurology overrides any intoxication explanation.

symptoms.symptom_name: Glasgow Coma Scale 13 (E3 V4 M6), clinician-measured and falling

diagnostic_weight_edges.diagnosis: expanding intracranial haemorrhage with raised ICP / early herniation
diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An abnormal, declining GCS objectively grades injury severity — a sealed clinician measurement; the AI Doctor infers decline from caregiver report, not a scored GCS.

symptoms.symptom_name: Hypertension with relative bradycardia (BP 165/95, HR 55) — Cushing reflex

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Hypertension with bradycardia is a late physiological signature of dangerously raised ICP — sealed clinician-measured vitals.

symptoms.symptom_name: Acute intracranial haemorrhage / midline shift / cerebral oedema on non-contrast CT head

investigation_details.expected_result: Acute intracranial haemorrhage with possible midline shift and/or cerebral oedema
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Missed, untreated expanding haematoma progressing to fatal herniation.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: CT is the definitive test that confirms the bleed and its subtype — only available after hospital transfer, never in telehealth.

symptom_clusters.target_diagnosis: expanding intracranial haemorrhage with raised ICP / early herniation
symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Declining conscious level + a fixed dilating pupil + Cushing reflex together define active uncal herniation demanding immediate neurosurgical transfer.
LOC, amnesia, repeated vomiting and falling GCS together mandate immediate imaging and emergency escalation under head-injury rules.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Definitive test for intracranial haemorrhage, midline shift and oedema; obtained after emergency transfer, never remotely.
Rapidly excludes hypoglycaemia as a contributor to altered consciousness in the prehospital/ED setting; does not delay transfer.
Guides reversal if bleeding; here reportedly not on anticoagulants, but confirmed in hospital.
High-energy fall with head strike mandates c-spine clearance alongside the head CT.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Skull / facial X-ray as a standalone test
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Plain skull films do not exclude intracranial haemorrhage and must not substitute for or delay CT and transfer.
